curated_content.name: TypeScript Best Practices
curated_content.source: TypeScript official documentation and community standards

readability_first.statement: Code is read more often than written - prioritize readability over cleverness

self_documenting.statement: Code should be self-documenting through clear naming and structure before relying on comments

variable_naming.title: Use Descriptive camelCase for Variables
variable_naming.description: Variables should use camelCase with descriptive names that clearly indicate their purpose
variable_naming.rationale: Descriptive camelCase is the TypeScript/JavaScript standard and makes code self-documenting
variable_naming.source: TypeScript style guide, Clean Code principles

when_applicable: Loop counters should still be descriptive
when_not_applicable: Private fields with underscore prefix (optional convention)

options.name: Descriptive camelCase
context_requirements: Boolean: prefix with is/has/should/can (e.g., isValid, hasPermission)
Arrays: plural names (e.g., users, items, products)
Objects: singular names (e.g., user, item, product)
Avoid abbreviations unless universally known (e.g., id, url, http)

decision_factors: Avoid single-letter names except simple loops (prefer index over i)

function_naming.title: Use Verb Phrases for Functions
function_naming.description: Functions should start with a verb that describes the action they perform
function_naming.rationale: Verb phrases clearly indicate what the function does, making code self-documenting

options.name: Verb-Noun Pattern
options.reason: Clear action indication, self-documenting, standard convention
context_requirements: CRUD operations: get/create/update/delete/remove
Boolean checks: is/has/should/can/validate/check
Data transformation: transform/convert/map/filter
Side effects: save/send/notify/log
Calculations: calculate/compute/determine

async_error_handling.title: Always Use Try-Catch for Async Operations
async_error_handling.description: All async operations must be wrapped in try-catch blocks to prevent unhandled promise rejections
async_error_handling.rationale: Unhandled promise rejections crash Node.js applications and create poor user experiences. Explicit error handling ensures graceful degradation

options.name: Try-Catch with Typed Error Handling
options.reason: Prevents crashes, enables proper error logging, provides user-friendly messages
context_requirements: Always catch errors in async functions
Log errors with context (user ID, operation, params)
Re-throw or return meaningful error responses
Use custom error classes for different error types
Include original error in context

decision_factors: Always log with context before throwing

custom_error_classes.title: Use Custom Error Classes for Different Error Types
custom_error_classes.description: Define custom error classes that extend Error to enable type-safe error handling and meaningful error messages
custom_error_classes.rationale: Custom error classes enable type-safe error handling, better error categorization, and more meaningful error messages

when_not_applicable: Errors that are immediately caught and handled

options.name: Custom Error Class Hierarchy
options.reason: Type-safe, enables instanceof checks, better error categorization

cons: Need to maintain error class hierarchy
Team needs to know about custom errors

context_requirements: Extend Error class
Include name, message, and optional context
Document when each error type is used
Examples: DatabaseError, ValidationError, NotFoundError, UnauthorizedError

jsdoc_requirement.title: JSDoc for All Public Functions and Complex Logic
jsdoc_requirement.description: All public functions and complex logic must have JSDoc comments with @param, @returns, and @throws tags
jsdoc_requirement.rationale: JSDoc provides IntelliSense support, auto-generated documentation, and helps other developers understand function contracts

options.name: Comprehensive JSDoc

pros: Better onboarding for new team members
cons: Can become outdated if not maintained

context_requirements: Include brief description
@param for each parameter with type and description
@returns with return type and description
@throws for any errors that can be thrown
@example for complex functions
Keep descriptions concise but clear

decision_factors: Complex logic → Always JSDoc with examples

prefer_functional.title: Prefer Functional Programming Over OOP When Appropriate
prefer_functional.description: Use pure functions and functional patterns for services, utilities, and React components unless OOP provides clear benefits
prefer_functional.rationale: Functional programming leads to more testable, predictable code with fewer side effects. TypeScript's type system works well with functional patterns
prefer_functional.source: Modern TypeScript best practices, React conventions

when_not_applicable: Complex state management (classes may be clearer)

options.name: Functional-First Approach
options.reason: More testable, fewer side effects, aligns with modern TypeScript/React

cons: May need to pass more parameters

context_requirements: Use functions with parameters over classes with state
Extract pure functions from methods
Use React hooks instead of class components
Prefer composition over inheritance
Use interfaces/types instead of classes when possible

options.name: Classes When Appropriate
options.reason: Classes provide clear encapsulation for complex stateful logic
context_requirements: Use when managing complex state
Use for services with lifecycle (init/cleanup)
Keep classes focused (single responsibility)
Prefer dependency injection over singletons

decision_factors: React UI → Functional components with hooks
Need lifecycle methods → Consider class or hooks

single_responsibility.title: Functions Should Do One Thing Well
single_responsibility.description: Each function should have a single, well-defined responsibility and be easy to name
single_responsibility.rationale: Single responsibility makes code easier to test, understand, modify, and reuse

options.name: Single Responsibility Functions
options.reason: Easier to test, understand, and maintain

pros: Easier to test (one thing to test)
Easier to modify without side effects

context_requirements: If function name includes 'and', consider splitting
If function is hard to name, it's doing too much
Function should fit on one screen (approx 20-30 lines)
Extract complex logic into helper functions
Each function should be easily testable in isolation

decision_factors: Hard to name → Doing too much, split it
Multiple levels of abstraction → Extract to helpers
Name includes 'and' → Split into two functions

import_order.title: Organize Imports in Consistent Order
import_order.description: Group and order imports for better readability and merge conflict reduction
import_order.rationale: Consistent import organization makes code easier to scan and reduces merge conflicts

options.name: Grouped and Sorted Imports
options.reason: Easier to scan, reduces merge conflicts, standard convention

pros: Easier to scan and find imports

context_requirements: Group 1: External libraries (node_modules)
Group 2: Internal shared libraries (@org/package)
Group 3: Relative parent imports (../..)
Group 4: Relative sibling imports (./)
Group 5: Type-only imports (import type)
Within groups: alphabetical order
Blank line between groups

decision_factors: Type imports → Last or grouped separately
Use ESLint import plugin for auto-fixing

test_coverage_requirement.title: Unit Tests for All Business Logic
test_coverage_requirement.description: All business logic, services, and utilities must have unit tests with minimum 80% coverage
test_coverage_requirement.rationale: Unit tests catch bugs early, enable confident refactoring, and serve as documentation
test_coverage_requirement.source: Testing best practices, team quality standards

options.name: Jest with 80% Coverage Minimum
options.reason: Catches bugs early, enables refactoring, serves as documentation

cons: Can give false confidence if poorly written

context_requirements: Test happy path
Test error conditions
Test edge cases
Test boundary conditions
Mock external dependencies
Use descriptive test names (should...)
Arrange-Act-Assert pattern

decision_factors: Aim for 80%+ coverage on critical modules

mandatory_technology_expert.title: MANDATORY: Consult Technology Expert Before Writing Code
mandatory_technology_expert.description: Before writing code in any language or technology, the appropriate technology expert (typescript-expert, csharp-expert, sql-server-expert, pdf-expert, etc.) MUST be consulted for syntax, best practices, and technology-specific patterns
mandatory_technology_expert.rationale: Technology experts contain authoritative facts (confidence 1.0) about language syntax, APIs, and best practices. Consulting them prevents syntax errors, anti-patterns, and ensures correct usage of language features

when_applicable: ALL code generation in ANY language
Framework-specific code → framework expert if available
when_not_applicable: Technology expert consultation is MANDATORY, not optional

options.name: Always Consult Technology Expert FIRST
options.reason: Technology experts provide authoritative facts (confidence 1.0) - this is not negotiable

cons: None - this is mandatory for quality code

context_requirements: BEFORE writing code, identify the technology/language
Load the appropriate technology expert (e.g., typescript-expert)
Apply expert's facts (confidence 1.0) for syntax and APIs
Apply expert's opinions for architectural decisions
If expert doesn't exist, use general knowledge but document this gap
ALWAYS cite which expert was consulted

decision_factors: Writing C# → MUST consult csharp-expert or dot-net-core-api-expert
Writing SQL Server queries → MUST consult sql-server-expert
Working with PDFs → MUST consult pdf-expert
Multiple technologies → Consult ALL relevant experts
No expert exists → Document gap, suggest creating expert

multi_expert_consultation.title: Consult ALL Relevant Experts for Multi-Domain Code
multi_expert_consultation.description: When writing code that spans multiple domains or technologies (e.g., TypeScript + PDF, C# + SQL Server), consult ALL relevant experts simultaneously and synthesize their recommendations
multi_expert_consultation.rationale: Complex code often touches multiple domains. Each expert provides authoritative knowledge for their domain. Consulting all relevant experts ensures the code is correct across all dimensions

when_not_applicable: Simple single-technology code (but still consult that one expert)

options.name: Multi-Expert Orchestration
options.reason: Ensures correctness across all domains involved in the code

pros: Leverages specialized knowledge from each domain
cons: May receive conflicting recommendations (resolve with context)

context_requirements: Identify ALL technologies/domains involved
Load ALL relevant experts
Example: TypeScript + PDF → Load typescript-expert AND pdf-expert
Example: C# API + SQL Server → Load csharp-expert AND sql-server-expert
Synthesize facts from all experts (all must be satisfied)
For opinions: Higher confidence expert takes precedence, OR more specific expert overrides general
Document which experts were consulted

decision_factors: Multiple domains → Consult ALL relevant experts
Conflicting opinions → Use context to decide, prefer more specific expert

analyze_before_creating.title: MANDATORY: Analyze Existing Code Before Creating New Solutions
analyze_before_creating.description: Before writing any new code, MUST search and analyze the existing codebase to determine if a solution already exists. Never recreate the wheel - reuse existing, tested code
analyze_before_creating.rationale: Duplicate code leads to maintenance nightmares, inconsistent behavior, increased bugs, and wasted effort. Analyzing first ensures we leverage existing solutions and maintain consistency
analyze_before_creating.source: DRY principle (Don't Repeat Yourself), software engineering best practices

when_applicable: Before adding API endpoints with similar functionality

options.name: Search-Analyze-Reuse Pattern
options.reason: Prevents code duplication, ensures consistency, leverages tested solutions

cons: Takes time to search and analyze
May need to adapt existing code slightly

context_requirements: STEP 1: Use codebase_search or grep to find similar functionality
Search for: similar function names, similar class names, similar patterns
Example: If creating 'getUserById', search for 'get.*ById', 'User.*Service', 'fetch.*User'
STEP 2: Read and analyze found code
Determine if existing code solves the problem
Check if existing code can be generalized/refactored for reuse
STEP 3: Choose action
REUSE: If exact match exists, use it directly
EXTEND: If close match, extend/generalize existing code
CREATE: Only if no similar solution exists, create new code
STEP 4: Document decision
If creating new code, document why existing solutions don't fit

decision_factors: Exact solution exists → REUSE it directly
Partial solution exists → EXTRACT common logic, reuse and extend
No solution exists → CREATE new code, but make it reusable
Always search with: codebase_search, grep, or file_search
Search keywords: function names, class names, domain terms
Before creating, ALWAYS justify why existing code doesn't work

build_reusable_solutions.title: Build Reusable Solutions, Not Hard-Coded Business Logic
build_reusable_solutions.description: All code should be designed for reusability and generalization. Avoid hard-coding specific business values, configurations, or logic. Instead, use parameters, configuration, and abstract patterns
build_reusable_solutions.rationale: Hard-coded solutions are brittle, difficult to test, and impossible to reuse. Reusable solutions reduce duplication, are easier to test, and adapt to changing requirements
build_reusable_solutions.source: Clean Architecture, SOLID principles, Domain-Driven Design

when_not_applicable: Configuration files (these SHOULD contain specific values)

options.name: Parameterized, Reusable Design
options.reason: Enables code reuse, easier testing, adapts to change, reduces duplication

pros: Code can be reused across multiple scenarios
cons: Need to identify the right abstractions

context_requirements: AVOID: Hard-coded strings, numbers, IDs, dates, business rules
INSTEAD: Accept as parameters, read from configuration, use constants
AVOID: Functions specific to one user, one product, one scenario
INSTEAD: Generalize to accept entity type, ID, filters as parameters
AVOID: Copy-paste code with slight variations
INSTEAD: Extract common logic, parameterize differences
USE: Dependency injection for services and repositories
USE: Configuration objects for business rules and thresholds
USE: Strategy pattern for varying algorithms
USE: Factory pattern for object creation
MAKE: Functions accept broad types (interfaces) not concrete classes
MAKE: Methods composable and chainable where appropriate

decision_factors: Hard-coded value → Move to parameter or configuration
Business rule → Extract to configurable strategy or policy
Specific entity ID → Generalize to accept any ID as parameter
Copy-paste code → Extract common function with parameters
One-off solution → Ask: How can this be generalized?
Before implementing → Ask: Will this work for other scenarios?
If function solves Problem X → Design it to solve Problem X for any Y
Example: Not 'getUserById(123)' → Instead 'getEntityById<T>(id: number, entityType: T)'
